Earth Science Exploration

Peru

Where the Andes meet the Amazon — a land shaped by ancient forces and living earth.

About This Project Explore Earth Processes

Machu Picchu, Cusco Region, Peru

Capital

Lima

Population

~33.4 million

Area

1,285,216 km²

Official Language

Spanish & Quechua

Historical & Cultural Overview

Peru is one of the world's great cradles of civilization — a land with over 5,000 years of continuous human history.

Historical Background

Peru is home to some of the oldest civilizations in the Americas, including the Norte Chico civilization (~3000 BCE) and the Inca Empire (1438–1533 CE) — the largest empire in pre-Columbian America, stretching from modern-day Colombia to Chile.

In 1532, Spanish conquistadors led by Francisco Pizarro arrived, beginning the colonial era. Peru declared independence from Spain on July 28, 1821. Today it is a democratic republic with a rich multicultural identity blending Indigenous, Spanish, African, Asian, and European influences.

Culture & Society

Peru's culture is deeply diverse. The country has 47 recognized Indigenous languages, with Quechua being the most widely spoken after Spanish. Traditional festivals like Inti Raymi (Festival of the Sun) and Carnival celebrate a vibrant mix of Catholic and Indigenous traditions.

Peruvian cuisine is internationally acclaimed — dishes like ceviche, lomo saltado, and causa reflect the cultural blending that defines the country. Lima is now considered one of the top culinary destinations in the world.

Physical Geography

Peru is divided into three major natural regions: the Costa (arid coastal desert), the Sierra (Andean highlands), and the Selva (Amazon rainforest). This geographic diversity gives Peru extraordinary biodiversity.

Peru contains ~10% of the world's plant species, is among the top 5 most biodiverse countries on Earth, and hosts the world's highest navigable lake — Lake Titicaca at 3,812 m above sea level.

Economy

Peru has one of Latin America's fastest-growing economies. It is one of the world's top producers of copper, gold, silver, zinc, and lead — all extracted from the Andes mountain range, making geology directly tied to national prosperity.

Other major sectors include agriculture (asparagus, blueberries, quinoa, coffee), tourism (Machu Picchu draws ~1.5 million visitors/year), and a growing tech and services sector centered in Lima.
